title: Performance Audit
prompt: Perform a design audit for a plugin that loads a dashboard script on every frontend page, calls a remote API on every page view, and stores logs in post meta. Return risks and fixes.
hints: Be concrete: which code path changes, which storage changes, and what gets cached?

model_answer.summary: The plugin is doing expensive work globally; load assets only where needed, cache remote API results, and move high-volume logs out of post meta.
model_answer.work: Only enqueue the dashboard script on the admin dashboard or the specific frontend route that needs it; do not enqueue it on every frontend page. Never call the remote API on every page view. Cache responses in a transient with a bounded TTL and serve stale data during short failures when acceptable. Replace post meta logs with a custom table designed for log queries, including indexes for created_at, status, and any lookup key. Review queries with Query Monitor and avoid unbounded meta_query scans on public traffic.
checks: Verify enqueue hooks load the asset only on the intended admin or frontend screen.
Test remote API caching and transient expiry.
Review custom table indexes against the expected query patterns.
Use Query Monitor to inspect query count and slow queries before and after the change.
Smoke test pages that do not need the dashboard script and confirm no asset is loaded.
model_answer.why_this_is_good: It connects each performance risk to a specific WordPress mechanism and a measurable verification step.
common_misses: Saying to optimize assets without naming enqueue scope.
Caching only successful remote API results without a failure plan.
Continuing to store high-volume logs in post meta.
model_answer.revision_prompt: Revise by naming the enqueue fix, remote API cache/transient strategy, custom table/index plan, query risk, and Query Monitor verification.

summary.description: One or two sentences explaining the answer.

work.description: The main implementation, review, or design answer.

checks.description: Verification steps, review checks, or acceptance criteria.
